metadata.description: C++ OOP: - `class`: A user-defined data type that can have methods and attributes. In Python, you define a class using the `class` keyword. - `public`: Access level for members of a class that can be accessed from anywhere outside the class. In Python, all members are public by default. - `protected`: Access level for members of a class that can only be accessed within the class and its subclasses. In Python, there is no built-in protected access level, but you can use a convention like prefixing with an underscore `_`. - `private`: Access level for members of a class that cannot be accessed outside the class. In Python, private members are typically indicated by prefixing with double underscores `__`. - `virtual function (vtable)`: A feature in C++ where functions can be overridden in derived classes and managed through a virtual table (`vtable`). This allows polymorphism. - `pure virtual function abstract class`: An abstract class in C++ that contains at least one pure virtual function, which must be implemented by any non-abstract subclass. In Python, this concept is not directly supported, but can be simulated using abstract base classes from the `abc` module. Python code example for a simple abstract class with a pure virtual function (using `abc`): ```python from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class AbstractClass(ABC): @abstractmethod def pure_virtual_function(self): pass # This will raise an error if you try to instantiate the AbstractClass directly. # a = AbstractClass() # Uncommenting this line will result in a TypeError. class ConcreteClass(AbstractClass): def pure_virtual_function(self): print("This function is implemented.") # You can now create instances of ConcreteClass c = ConcreteClass() c.pure_virtual_function() ``` Note: The concept of `virtual` functions and `vtable` is more relevant to C++ than Python, where method overriding is achieved through inheritance.

data_pointers.content_preview: C++ OOP: - `class`: A user-defined data type that can have methods and attributes. In Python, you define a class using the `class` keyword. - `public`: Access level for members of a class that can be accessed from anywhere outside the class. In Python, all members are public by default. - `protected`: Access level for members of a class that can only be accessed within the class and its subclasses. In Python, there is no built-in protected access level, but you can use a convention like prefixing with an underscore `_`. - `private`: Access level for members of a class that cannot be accessed outside the class. In Python, private members are typically indicated by prefixing with double underscores `__`. - `virtual function (vtable)`: A feature in C++ where functions can be overridden in derived classes and managed through a virtual table (`vtable`). This allows polymorphism. - `pure virtual function abstract class`: An abstract class in C++ that contains at least one pure virtual function, which must be implemented by any non-abstract subclass. In Python, this concept is not directly supported, but can be simulated using abstract base classes from the `abc` module. Python code example for a simple abstract class with a pure virtual function (using `abc`): ```python from abc import ABC, abstractmethod class AbstractClass(ABC): @abstractmethod def pure_virtual_function(self): pass # This will raise an error if you try to instantiate the AbstractClass directly. # a = AbstractClass() # Uncommenting this line will result in a TypeError. class ConcreteClass(AbstractClass): def pure_virtual_function(self): print("This function is implemented.") # You can now create instances of ConcreteClass c = ConcreteClass() c.pure_virtual_function() ``` Note: The concept of `virtual` functions and `vtable` is more relevant to C++ than Python, where method overriding is achieved through inheritance.

common_pitfalls: 忘记声明虚析构函数：当基类指针指向派生类对象时，若基类析构函数非虚，delete 时不会调用派生类析构函数，导致资源泄漏。
对象切片：将派生类对象按值传递给接受基类对象的函数，会丢失派生类特有成员，应使用指针或引用传递。
在构造函数或析构函数中调用虚函数：此时虚函数机制不按预期工作，因为派生类部分尚未构造或已销毁，应避免此类调用。
